examples.en: I am going to the market.

examples.kp: Sali ku ning prutas king tindahan.
examples.en: I will buy fruit at the store.

examples.kp: Magkanu ya ing libro king tindahan?
examples.en: How much is the book at the store?

entries.notes: The primary locative/oblique particle in Kapampangan, equivalent to Tagalog 'sa'. Marks location ('in/at'), direction ('to/into'), recipient ('for'), and oblique nouns generally. It is the oblique counterpart of 'ing' (absolutive) and 'ning' (genitive). Extremely high frequency — appears in virtually every sentence with a locative complement. Confidence: very high; attested extensively in grammars (Bergaño tradition) and all modern sources.

examples.kp: Kailangan ku ning gamut para king sakit ku.
examples.en: I need medicine for my illness.

examples.kp: Atin ku pung payung para king uran.
examples.en: I have an umbrella for the rain.

entries.notes: Spanish loanword (para) fully integrated into Kapampangan. Used to mark purpose ('in order to') or beneficiary ('for'). Often precedes king + noun phrase. Confidence: very high.

examples.kp: Makilabas ku pu, munta ku na.
examples.en: Excuse me, I am leaving now.

entries.notes: Politeness/respect enclitic, equivalent to Tagalog 'po'. Used when addressing elders, strangers, or people of higher status. Often appears fused as 'pung' (pu + ng ligature) before vowel-initial words: e.g., 'Atin ku pung payung'. Confidence: very high; widely attested.

examples.en: My younger sibling is still little.
Go to the store again / also.

entries.notes: Aspectual/modal enclitic particle. Indicates continuation of a state ('still'), incompleteness ('yet'), or addition ('also/more'). Cognate with and functions similarly to Tagalog 'pa'. Confidence: very high.

examples.kp: Nung ati kang sakit, munta ka king doktor.
examples.en: If you are sick, go to the doctor.
There are many stars when it is night.
I am sad when you leave.

entries.notes: Conditional/temporal subordinating conjunction. Equivalent to Tagalog 'kung' (if) or 'kapag/pag' (when). Used for both hypothetical conditions and habitual/temporal clauses. Confidence: very high; attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.kp: Malungkut ing anak uling wala ne ing asawa na.
examples.en: The child is sad because his parent is gone.

examples.kp: Marine ya uling binura ne ing sulat na.
examples.en: She is embarrassed because her letter was erased.

examples.kp: Marirya ya uling dakal a lugud ku para king anak.
examples.en: She is touched because I have great love for the child.

entries.notes: Causal subordinating conjunction meaning 'because/since'. Equivalent to Tagalog 'kasi' or 'dahil'. Introduces the reason or cause clause. Confidence: very high; directly attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.en: I want to go to the store.
I want to go to the mountain.

entries.notes: Stative verb/modal meaning 'to want, to like'. Takes the genitive enclitic ku (I) or mu (you) when the wanter is subject. Very commonly followed by an infinitive verb phrase. Attested in existing pack sentences and the Wikipedia Kapampangan article ('Isanu deti ya ing bisa ka?'). Confidence: very high.

examples.kp: Malyari mu lang palitan dening pera ku?
examples.en: Can you exchange my money for me?

entries.notes: 2nd-person singular genitive/non-subject (ng-set) enclitic pronoun. Marks possession ('your') or the actor of a non-topic action ('by you'). Equivalent to Tagalog 'mo'. Not to be confused with the particle 'mu' meaning 'only/just', which is a separate homophone sometimes noted in older Kapampangan texts but rare in modern speech. Confidence: very high.

examples.en: I will give the book to him/her.

entries.notes: Portmanteau pronoun combining 1st-singular actor (ku) and 3rd-singular object (ya). The form 'ke' is the reduced/fused enclitic that appears when both a 1sg actor and a 3sg patient are expressed together, analogous to Tagalog 'ko' (actor) + 'siya/ito' context. Some analyses derive it as ku + ya → ke by vowel coalescence. Listed in Kapampangan grammar references as a lexicalized enclitic cluster. Confidence: high — directly attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.kp: Dakal a pagmaragul king puso ku para keka.
examples.en: There is great pride in my heart for you.

entries.notes: Oblique (sa-set) 2nd-person singular pronoun. Equivalent to Tagalog 'sa iyo'. Used as indirect object ('to you'), recipient ('for you'), or other oblique roles. Contrast with absolutive 'ka' (you, topic) and genitive 'mu' (your). Confidence: very high; widely attested.

examples.kp: Mayap ya ing Ingkung ku kaku.
examples.en: My grandfather is good to me.

examples.kp: Matula ku king lugud mu para kaku.
examples.en: I am happy about your love for me.
My aunt is the one who called me.

entries.notes: Oblique (sa-set) 1st-person singular pronoun. Equivalent to Tagalog 'sa akin'. Also sometimes written or pronounced 'kanaku'. Used as indirect object ('to me'), recipient ('for me'), or other oblique roles. Confidence: very high; directly attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.en: I will give the book to him/her.
I am writing a letter to him/her.
I have great love for her.

entries.notes: Oblique (sa-set) 3rd-person singular pronoun. Equivalent to Tagalog 'sa kanya'. Also found as 'keya' in some dialect/register variation, but 'kaniya' is the standard modern form. Used as indirect object or other oblique role. Confidence: very high; attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.kp: Ibie ke ing susi kang Carmen.
examples.en: I will give the key to Carmen.
I am writing a letter to Father.

examples.kp: Ibiye mu kang Pedro ing kwalta.

entries.notes: Oblique personal article used before proper names (personal names, kinship terms used as names). Equivalent to Tagalog 'kay'. Marks the oblique (indirect object, beneficiary, location) role of a named person. Contrast with 'king' (oblique for common nouns) and 'kang' as a contraction of 'ka + ng' ligature (a separate use). Confidence: high.

examples.kp: Atin libre a pamangan kareng anak.
examples.en: There is free food for the children.
I am full from the rice (among/of the rice).

examples.kp: Ibie me kareng tau ing pamangan.
examples.en: Give the food to the people.

entries.notes: Oblique (sa-set) plural article. Equivalent to Tagalog 'sa mga'. Used when the oblique noun phrase is plural and is a common noun. Corresponds to the absolutive plural 'reng/ding'. Confidence: high; attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.en: There is fire here, let us be careful.

examples.kp: Atin ku pung payung para king uran.
examples.en: I have an umbrella for the rain.
There are many stones in the river.

entries.notes: Existential verb/particle meaning 'there is/are' or 'to have'. Equivalent to Tagalog 'mayroon/meron'. When combined with a pronoun enclitic (e.g., atin ku = I have), it expresses possession. Negated by 'ala' (there is not). Confidence: very high; extensively attested.

examples.kp: Atyu ya king lalam ning lamesa.
Atyu ya ing kotsi king arap ning bale.
examples.en: The car is in front of the house.
The food is on the table.

entries.notes: Locative existential verb/predicate. While 'atin' asserts existence ('there is'), 'atyu' specifies location ('is located at/in'). Equivalent to Tagalog 'nandoon/narito/nasa'. Requires a locative complement marked by 'king'. Confidence: very high; extensively attested in existing packs.

Proximal demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this' (near the speaker). Equivalent to Tagalog 'ito'. Functions as a noun substitute. Attested in Wikipedia ('Makananu iti gawan?') and widely in other sources. Contrast with 'iyan' (that, near addressee) and 'ita' (that, far from both). Confidence: very high.

Proximal demonstrative, closely related to 'iti'. 'Ini' typically functions as a post-nominal modifier ('the bag ini' = 'this bag'), while 'iti' is more often used as a sentence-level predicative or object pronoun. The distinction in modern Kapampangan is not always sharp; both translate to 'this'. Attested in existing pack sentences ('Kaninu ya ing bag ini?'). Confidence: high.
Limiter particle — restricts scope to 'only' or 'just'. Shared with Tagalog. Common in everyday speech to soften requests or limit quantities: 'Metung lang' (just one), 'Munta lang' (just go).

'me' is the 2nd-person actor enclitic — it marks 'you' as the one performing the action. It follows the verb directly: 'Sara me' (you close it), 'Linisan me' (you clean it), 'Ibuklat me' (you open it). Contrast with 'mu' (2sg non-subject/possessive: 'ing bale mu' = your house).

examples.kp: Isanu deti ya ing bisa ka?
examples.en: Which of these do you want?

entries.notes: Proximal plural demonstrative pronoun meaning 'these'. Equivalent to Tagalog 'mga ito'. The plural counterpart of 'iti/ini'. Attested in Wikipedia ('Isanu deti ya ing bisa ka?') and in existing pack sentences. Confidence: very high.

examples.en: It was dropped to the ground.

examples.kp: Matula la ngan keng kasuyuan king aldo na ita.
examples.en: They were all joyful in the happiness of that day.

entries.notes: Pre-vocalic allomorph of 'king' — used before vowel-initial words exactly as English 'an' is used before vowels. 'King' and 'keng' are the same particle; the choice is purely phonological: 'king bale' (at home) but 'keng eskwela' (at school). Both translate to the Tagalog locative 'sa'.

examples.kp: Sulud me ing sako mu ta marimla.
examples.en: Put on your jacket because it is cold.

examples.kp: Sulud me ing payung ta miguran na.
examples.en: Use the umbrella since it is already raining.

entries.notes: Causal subordinating conjunction — introduces a reason or cause clause, equivalent to 'because' or 'since'. It follows the main clause: '[imperative], ta [reason]'. Distinct from the inclusive-we enclitic 'tamu' (let's go) and the noun 'ta' (father, in some dialects). In the particles context it always marks causation.

examples.kp: Meniglo la ngan king gewa na.
examples.en: They were all astonished at what he did.

entries.notes: Adversative/concessive particle — follows the first clause/predicate and introduces a contrasting or unexpected result. In 'Dukha ya ngan masaya ya' it means 'but'. In collective/emphatic contexts ('Meniglo la ngan') it means 'even so/after all/altogether'. Equivalent to Tagalog 'pero' (adversative) or 'kahit na' (concessive) depending on context.

Discourse particle/interjection expressing agreement or permission — 'okay', 'go ahead', 'alright'. Shared with Tagalog, from Spanish 'sigue' (continue). Used at the start of a response or to give approval.

Existential negator/modifier meaning 'without' or 'there is no [X]'. It is the linker form of 'ala' (there is none) used as an attributive modifier before a noun: 'alang parsala' (without fault), 'alang pera' (without money). Equivalent to Tagalog 'walang'. Compare 'ala' (predicate: 'Ala ku kuwarta' = I have no money) vs 'alang-' (modifier before a noun).

examples.kp: Malupit ya ing dalan papunta king bunduk.
examples.en: The path going to the mountain is rough.

entries.notes: Directional particle meaning 'going to' or 'toward a destination'. Always followed by 'king/keng + destination'. Used both as a verb complement ('lumakad papunta king bale' = walked toward the house) and as a postmodifier of a noun phrase ('dalan papunta king bunduk' = path to the mountain). Shared with Tagalog.

examples.kp: Kailangan ku ning gamut para king sakit ku.
examples.en: I need medicine for my illness.

examples.kp: Kailangan ku ing kulambung para king yamuk.
examples.en: I need a mosquito net for the mosquitoes.

entries.notes: Modal predicate expressing necessity or need — 'to need', 'it is necessary'. Shared with Tagalog (from Malay 'kailangan'). Follows predicate-initial word order: 'Kailangan ku [ing/ning object]'. Compare 'bisa' (to want/can) and 'uling' (because/reason). Widely used in modern Kapampangan speech.

Conjunction meaning 'and / also / together with'. A native Kapampangan word, equivalent to 'at' (which is more common in modern speech). 'ampo' appears frequently in traditional Kapampangan texts and prayers. In everyday speech, 'at' and 'ampo' are interchangeable.

Epistemic modal particle expressing uncertainty or conjecture — 'I think', 'probably', 'it seems'. Placed after the predicate phrase. Shared with Tagalog (where it carries the same meaning). In Kapampangan it softens a statement to indicate the speaker is not fully certain.

Particle indicating that something has vanished, run out, or is no longer present. Predicates the absence/disappearance of the subject: 'Wari kwalta na' (The money is gone/used up). Used for things that have been consumed, lost, or left. Distinct from 'ala' (there is none — simple absence) vs 'wari' (was there but is gone now).

examples.kp: Malaus ko pu, mangan kamu deni!

entries.notes: Proximal locative adverb meaning 'here' — the locative/oblique counterpart of the demonstratives 'ini/iti'. Used sentence-finally or after a verb to indicate location near the speaker. 'Dening' is the form with the 'ing' article fused: 'palitan dening pera' (exchange this money here). Equivalent to Tagalog 'dito'.

Noun meaning 'fault, blame, sin, wrongdoing'. Derived from the root 'sala' (mistake/fault) with the Kapampangan nominalizing prefix 'par-'. Used in moral and religious contexts: 'alang parsala' (without fault/blameless), 'malagung parsala' (a heavy sin). Equivalent to Tagalog 'sala' or 'pagkakasala' in a moral sense.

examples.kp: Marine ya uling binura ne ing sulat na.
examples.en: He/she is sad because his/her letter was already erased.

entries.notes: Resultative/completive particle meaning 'already done, now in that state, no more'. A portmanteau/coalescence of 'na' (already/now) + 'ya' (3sg enclitic) — thus 'ne' is often found where 'na ya' would occur in more careful speech. Example: 'Matua na ya' → 'Matua ne'. Equivalent to Tagalog 'na na siya' → 'na na sya'. Very high frequency in everyday speech. Also used standalone as 'wala ne' (gone/no more).

First-person inclusive hortative particle — invites both speaker and listener to do something together ('let us/let's'). A coalescence of 'ta' (1pl inclusive) + 'na' (now/already). Used after the verb: 'Munta tana' (Let's go), 'Mangan tana' (Let's eat). Equivalent to Tagalog 'tayo na'. Highly frequent in spoken Kapampangan. Distinct from 'taku' (we/let's in actor position).

Emphatic enclitic with two related functions: (1) emphatic intensifier meaning 'really, indeed, truly' — 'Maragul ya namu' (It is really big); (2) first-person exclusive plural possessive 'ours' — 'asu namu' (our dog). Context distinguishes the two. As an intensifier it is post-predicate. As possessive it follows the noun. Compare 'namu' (our) vs 'mi' (our — subject form).

Discourse particle used to introduce a reciprocal or contrasting element. Four main uses: (1) reciprocal — 'Mayap ka naman' (And you?); (2) 'you're welcome' in the set phrase 'Alang nanu naman' (It's nothing / You're welcome); (3) additive 'also/too'; (4) mild contrast 'on the other hand'. Shared with Tagalog where 'naman' carries the same functions. Extremely frequent in conversation.

examples.kp: Ali ka matakut; kayabe ku ka.
examples.en: Don't be afraid; I am with you.

entries.notes: Preposition/particle meaning 'with, together with, accompanied by'. Equivalent to Tagalog 'kasama'. Used before or after the noun phrase it governs: 'Kayabe ku ka' (I am with you — I accompany you) or 'Munta taku kayabe' (Let's go together). A key word for expressing accompaniment and solidarity.

examples.kp: Mangan na tamu, tapos munta na tamu.
examples.en: Let us eat, then let us go.

entries.notes: Functions both as a conjunction ('then, after that') linking sequential actions and as an adjective/predicate ('finished, done'). Though 'tapos' appears in both Kapampangan and Tagalog, it is in common everyday use in Kapampangan speech. Used to sequence events or signal completion.

lessons.title: Key Particles: king, para, pu, pa, nung, uling, bisa
lessons.intro: Kapampangan has a set of particles and conjunctions that appear in almost every sentence. The locative particle 'king' (= Tagalog 'sa') is the most common word in the language — it marks location, direction, and oblique nouns. 'Para' (for/in order to) marks purpose. 'Pu' adds politeness when speaking to elders. 'Pa' means 'still/yet/more'. 'Nung' introduces 'if/when' clauses, and 'uling' means 'because'. 'Bisa' is the main verb for wanting or liking something.

exercises.prompt: Which word means "to want" or "to like"?
What does "pu" signal in a sentence?

accepted: i am going to the market
i am going to the market.
i will go to the market
i will go to the market.

exercises.prompt: Nung ati kang sakit, munta ka king doktor.
accepted: if you are sick, go to the doctor
if you are sick, go to the doctor.
when you are sick, go to the doctor
when you are sick, go to the doctor.
whenever you are sick, go to the doctor
whenever you are sick, go to the doctor.

lessons.title: Oblique Pronouns: mu, ke, keka, kaku, kaniya, kang, kareng
lessons.theme: The sa-set (oblique) pronouns and genitive enclitic
lessons.intro: Kapampangan has a full set of oblique pronouns — equivalents of Tagalog 'sa akin, sa iyo, sa kanya'. These mark indirect objects, beneficiaries, and other non-topic roles. 'Mu' is the 2nd-singular genitive enclitic meaning 'your' or 'by you'. 'Ke' is a portmanteau combining I + it/him/her into a single enclitic. 'Keka' means 'to/for you', 'kaku' means 'to/for me', and 'kaniya' means 'to/for him or her'. For proper names, 'kang' is used (= Tagalog 'kay'). For plural nouns, 'kareng' is the oblique plural article.

exercises.prompt: How do you say "to you / for you" (singular) in Kapampangan?

accepted: i will give the book to him
i will give the book to him.
i will give the book to her
i will give the book to her.
i will give the book for him
i will give the book for him.
i will give the book for her
i will give the book for her.
i will give the book him/her (oblique)
i will give the book him/her (oblique).

exercises.prompt: Ibie ke ing susi ____ Carmen. (to — before a name)

lessons.title: Existentials & Demonstratives: atin, atyu, iti, ini, deti
lessons.intro: Two verbs cover existence and location in Kapampangan. 'Atin' (there is/are; to have) is the general existential — equivalent to Tagalog 'mayroon'. 'Atyu' (is located at) is the locative existential that always pairs with the particle 'king' to specify where. For pointing out things nearby, Kapampangan uses three demonstratives: 'iti' (this — used as a pronoun), 'ini' (this — used as a modifier after the noun), and 'deti' (these — plural). These words let learners point at and identify objects in conversation.

accepted: there is fire here, let us be careful
there is fire here, let us be careful.
there is fire here, we must be careful
there is fire here, we must be careful.

exercises.prompt: Atyu ya king lalam ning lamesa.
Kaninu ya ing bag ____? (this — modifier form)

lessons.title: You as the Actor: me
lessons.intro: The enclitic 'me' marks the 2nd person ('you') as the actor — the one performing the action. It comes right after the verb: 'Sara me' (close it), 'Linisan me' (clean it). This is one of the most common patterns in everyday Kapampangan speech, especially for requests and instructions. Contrast with 'mu' (your / by you — used for possession and non-topic roles).

exercises.prompt: What does 'me' signal in a sentence?

lessons.title: Core Pronouns: ya and ku
lessons.intro: Two enclitics you will hear in almost every Kapampangan sentence. 'ya' marks the 3rd person singular topic (he/she/it) and follows the predicate: 'Maragul ya' (It is big). 'ku' marks the 1st person singular topic (I/me) and also expresses possession: 'bale ku' (my house). When either is followed by a linker '-ng', they fuse: ya + ng = 'yang', ku + ng = 'kung'.

exercises.prompt: Masipag ____ ing anak. (The child is hardworking.)

lessons.title: Aspect and Limiter: na and lang
lessons.intro: Two of the most useful little words in Kapampangan. 'na' signals that something has already happened or is happening now: 'Munta ku na' (I am going now / I am already leaving). 'lang' means 'only' or 'just' and limits or softens a statement: 'Metung lang' (just one). A special fusion: when 'na' immediately precedes 'ya', the pair contracts to 'ne': 'Gutom ne' (He/she is already hungry).

exercises.prompt: Metung ____ anak da. (They have only one child.)

lessons.title: Location and Cause: keng and ta
lessons.intro: 'keng' is simply 'king' before a vowel-initial word — the same locative particle, just shaped by the following sound. 'ta' links a command or statement to its reason: 'Sulud me ing sako mu ta marimla' (Put on your jacket because it is cold).

accepted: it was dropped to the ground
it was dropped to the ground.
it was dropped on the ground
it was dropped on the ground.

exercises.prompt: Sulud me ing sako mu ta marimla.
accepted: put on your jacket because it is cold
put on your jacket because it is cold.
put on your jacket since it is cold
put on your jacket since it is cold.
wear your jacket because it is cold
wear your jacket because it is cold.
wear your jacket since it is cold
wear your jacket since it is cold.

exercises.prompt: Mikit tana ____ tutuki! (See you next time!)
Sulud me ing payung ____ miguran na. (Use the umbrella since it is already raining.)

lessons.title: More Particles: ngan, sige, alang, papunta, kailangan
lessons.theme: contrast, agreement, negation, direction, and need
lessons.intro: 'ngan' contrasts two ideas (poor but happy). 'Sige' gives agreement or permission (okay/go ahead). 'alang' means 'without/there is no'. 'papunta' marks direction toward a place. 'Kailangan' means 'to need' and starts a necessity sentence.

exercises.prompt: What does 'ngan' mean in 'Dukha ya ngan masaya ya'?
Kailangan ku ning gamut para king sakit ku.
accepted: i need medicine for my illness
i need medicine for my illness.
i need medicine for my sickness
i need medicine for my sickness.

exercises.prompt: Tikdo yang ____ parsala. (He stands without fault.)
Lumakad ya ____ king bale. (He walked toward the house.)

lessons.title: Conjunctions, Mood, and Locative Here
lessons.intro: 'ampo' means 'and/also'. 'yata' expresses uncertainty: 'I think, probably'. 'wari' signals something is gone or used up. 'deni' means 'here' (at this place). 'parsala' is a noun meaning fault or sin.

exercises.prompt: Bisa kung nasi ____ asan. (I want rice and fish.)
Malaus ko pu, mangan kamu ____! (Welcome, please eat here!)

lessons.title: Resultative, Hortative, and Social Particles
lessons.intro: 'ne' = already/now (resultative particle, often na+ya fused). 'tana' = let's (1pl inclusive hortative). 'namu' = really/indeed or our (emphatic). 'naman' = also/you're welcome/in return. 'kayabe' = with, together with.

exercises.prompt: Ali ka matakut; kayabe ku ka.
accepted: don't be afraid; i am with you
don't be afraid; i am with you.
don't be afraid, i am with you
don't be afraid, i am with you.
do not be afraid; i am with you
do not be afraid; i am with you.

exercises.prompt: Munta ____ king simbahan. (Let's go to church.)
Maragul ya ing asu ____. (Our dog is really big.)
Mayap ka ____! (And you! / You're fine too!)

lessons.title: Sequence & Completion: tapos
lessons.intro: 'Tapos' functions both as a conjunction ('then/after') linking sequential actions and as a predicate ('finished/done'). Though shared with Tagalog, it is in common everyday use in Kapampangan speech.
